text: The surgeon opens the cyst located in the posterior part of the left broad ligament, revealing and removing the dark chocolate-colored blood from inside the cyst.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, removing pathological attachments between the ovary and the posterior part of the broad ligament. The abdominal cavity is rinsed with fluid in preparation for the cystectomy procedure.
The laparoscopic view reveals the anatomical structures including the left fallopian tube, the infundibulopelvic ligament, the round ligament, and the left ovary.
The video shows an abnormal attachment between the ovary and the utero-ovarian ligament.
The surgeon visually identifies the utero-ovarian ligament, highlighting its anatomical position in relation to the ovary and uterus.
The surgeon proceeds with the cystectomy after freeing the ovary from its pathological attachment, aiming to remove the visible cyst from the ovary.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the ovary to preserve the ovarian follicle, identifying the plane between the ovarian cortex and the endometriotic cyst for precise dissection.
The surgeon identifies level one, where there is a less dense adhesion between the ovarian cortex and the cyst, facilitating easier separation.
The surgeon navigates deeper into the surgical field, revealing the ovarian cortex and adjacent structures, while carefully avoiding severe attachments to prevent false dissection.
The surgeon carefully performs a bloodless dissection between the ovary and the ovarian cortex, aiming to minimize bleeding while separating these structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection between the ovarian cortex and the endometriotic cyst, maintaining a bloodless plane by carefully navigating the tissue layers.

The surgeon uses forceps to apply millimetric traction on the tissue, carefully repositioning the instrument to maintain optimal traction and achieve a bloodless dissection between the ovarian cortex and the cyst.
The surgeon identifies zone two of the cyst, which exhibits more adhesions compared to zone one, requiring careful dissection to separate the cyst from surrounding tissues.
As the surgeon progresses deeper into the cyst, the risk of damaging the ovarian cortex increases, highlighting the need for careful dissection.
The surgeon visually identifies the cyst within the surgical field, highlighting its presence in relation to the surrounding ovarian tissue.
The surgeon visually identifies the cyst, highlighting its presence within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the ovarian cortex adjacent to the cyst, highlighting its proximity during the dissection.
The cyst is visible in the surgical field, with its structure clearly delineated against the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies a bleeding site within the cyst, indicating the location as number two.
The cyst is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the ovarian cortex, revealing the cyst. The surgical plane is visible as the dissection progresses deeper into the ovary.

The video shows the ovarian cortex with a visible cyst in the middle. The surgeon identifies the plane for dissection in zone three, which is noted to have dense adhesions, requiring careful and deliberate work.
The surgeon performs a bloodless dissection of the cyst, gently retracting the ovarian cortex while frequently repositioning the forceps to maintain effective traction.

The surgeon repositions the forceps to work on the ovary, attempting to navigate the dissection plane. The ovary is visible, and the surgical team encounters some difficulty in maintaining the correct plane.

The surgeon struggles to clearly identify the cleavage plane between the cyst and the ovary, adjusting the traction angle to minimize trauma. Bleeding is observed, and energy devices are avoided to prevent further complications.
The surgeon places forceps on the bleeding site to control hemorrhage and continues applying traction to manipulate the tissue.
The surgeon identifies a bleeder in the surgical field, likely near the ovarian cortex and cyst, requiring careful management to maintain a bloodless dissection.
The surgeon uses bipolar energy for hemostasis and prepares to place a monocryl stitch in the ovary, handing the bleeder to the assistant.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 Monocryl stitch to reconstruct the normal anatomy of the ovary, beginning the suturing process from the middle part of the ovary to minimize the risk of abnormal adhesion.
The surgeon tracks the needle through the ovarian tissue using a monofilament stitch, demonstrating the ease of suturing with this type of stitch.
The surgeon places a stitch in the cortical part of the ovary, aiming to invaginate the ovary and reduce the risk of ovarian adhesion.
The surgeon manipulates the cut ovarian cortex, attempting to invaginate the previous cyst bed. A stitch is placed without locking the knot, aiming for a loose closure to minimize tension.
The surgeon completes the ovarian reconstruction by tying the final knot, ensuring it is secure but not overly tight to avoid ischemia of the ovaries.
The surgeon uses the entracofoil knotting technique to lock the knot on the ovarian cortex, ensuring secure closure while reconstructing the ovary to minimize the risk of adhesion.
The surgeon completes hemostasis of the ovary using monocryl sutures, tying three or four knots to secure the tissue.
The surgeon visually inspects the surgical field to identify any additional areas of endometriosis localization within the ovarian tissue.
The surgeon demonstrates that the small cyst can be extracted through the trocar without the need for an endo bag.
The surgeon extracts the small cyst directly through the trocar without using an endo bag, demonstrating the cyst's size and the efficiency of the extraction method.
The surgeon irrigates the surgical area while extracting the cyst, ensuring the removal site is clean and clear of debris.
The surgical field is shown post-cystectomy, revealing the anatomical structures after the removal of the cyst.
The surgeon examines the posterior part of the left broad ligament following the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to control hemostasis, ensuring that any bleeding is managed effectively following the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon observes an active lesion characterized by visible bleeding within the surgical field.
The ureter is visible in the surgical field, identifiable as a tubular structure running along the posterior part of the brown ligament.
